question: What lines make up the RCS piping outside the RPV?

nuscale_ch05_0021.title: Reactor Coolant System Piping
nuscale_ch05_0021.summary: The section discusses the design and degradation assessment of the NuScale reactor coolant system's steam generator (SG) tubing. It notes that the main degradation observed in similar operating reactors is wear from flow-induced vibration or foreign objects, and that NuScale's design—with its helical tubing and novel support structure—does not increase risk compared to existing designs. Prototypic testing and standard SG program guidelines are deemed sufficient, with wear identified as the most likely degradation mechanism and secondary corrosion risks considered low.

nuscale_ch05_0018.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0018.summary: This section outlines the requirements and procedures for the inspection, shipment, installation, operating conditions, inservice surveillance, and threaded fasteners of the NuScale reactor vessel, referencing relevant ASME and ASTM standards. It specifies the use of nondestructive examinations, controlled shipping environments, and quality assurance measures to ensure the integrity and safety of the reactor vessel. Key standards cited include the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code and ASME NQA-1 for quality assurance.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module's reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a key pressure-retaining component and barrier to fission product release, supporting various reactor systems and constructed according to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards. The materials used for the RPV are carefully selected to ensure integrity and compliance with regulatory requirements throughout the plant's design lifetime.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes key components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. It removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining pressure boundary integrity to prevent radionuclide release. The RCS interfaces with several other systems, including emergency core cooling and chemical control, to ensure safe and efficient operation.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0007.title: Integrity of Reactor Coolant Boundary
nuscale_ch05_0007.summary: This section describes the design features of each NuScale Power Module (NPM) that ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) throughout the plant’s design life, in accordance with regulatory definitions. It details the components and systems included in the RCPB, such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment isolation valves, safety valves, and associated piping, emphasizing their roles in maintaining pressure containment and system safety.

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report regarding the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary. It includes guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as tables summarizing relevant ASME code cases for reactor components.

edges.description: Section B describes the physical boundaries of the RCS piping as being located between the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and the containment vessel (CNV), which provides context for identifying which lines make up the RCS piping outside the RPV as asked in the analysis question, while Section A discusses the design and degradation considerations for RCS piping components such as steam generator tubing.

Section B lists specific lines such as the degasification line and reactor vent valves that are part of the RCS piping outside the RPV, providing concrete examples that Section A's general discussion of RCS piping does not enumerate.

Section B states that the NuScale design does not include an external RCS piping system, which directly addresses the analysis question and provides context for interpreting the details in Section A about RCS components.

Section B states that the NuScale design does not include an external RCS piping system, directly addressing the analysis question, while Section A discusses inspection and handling requirements for the RPV but does not specify any external RCS piping, thus Section B's information clarifies and limits the scope implied in Section A.

Section A describes the physical boundaries and components of the RCS piping outside the RPV, while Section B specifies the functional lines (such as the degasification line and reactor vent valves) that make up the RCS piping outside the RPV, thus providing details for Section A's general description.

Section A references the RCS piping between the RPV and CNV, while Section B specifies that the RCS does not include an external piping system, indicating that the RCS piping outside the RPV is not present in this design.

Section A describes specific lines (degasification line and reactor vent valves) associated with the RCS outside the RPV, while Section B establishes that the NuScale RCS design does not include an external piping system, thus providing a general design basis that frames the context for interpreting the lines mentioned in Section A.

Section A defines the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) as including pressure-retaining components of the RCS up to and including the outermost containment isolation valve, while Section B discusses the design and degradation mechanisms of RCS piping, specifically steam generator tubing, which is part of the RCS outside the reactor pressure vessel (RPV); thus, Section B provides details about the components referenced in Section A's boundary definition.

Section A defines the extent of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), specifying that it includes RCS piping up to and including the outermost containment isolation valve, while Section B discusses requirements and procedures for the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) itself, thus Section A's boundary definition structurally specifies the interface point relevant to Section B's focus on the RPV.

Section A defines the extent of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) up to the outermost containment isolation valve, while Section B describes the physical configuration of the reactor vessel and the piping between the RPV and the containment vessel, thus specifying which lines are considered part of the RCS piping outside the RPV.

Section A provides references and standards that underpin the technical and regulatory basis for the design and integrity of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping discussed in Section B, which describes the design and operational considerations of the RCS piping outside the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV).

Section A and Section B are related because both reference the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report and ASME NQA-1-2008/1a-2009 Addenda, indicating that the quality assurance requirements for RCS piping outside the RPV and for the RPV itself are governed by the same regulatory standards.

Section A provides references to regulatory and technical standards relevant to the integrity and quality assurance of the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B describes the physical configuration of the reactor vessel and associated piping, connecting the referenced standards in A to the actual system components discussed in B.

Section A describes the components included in the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), which is relevant to identifying the RCS piping outside the RPV, while Section B lists references that support or provide background for the technical and regulatory basis of Section A's descriptions.
